The problem description says "A rotation (fold) at node n consists of one action rotate and two following passes...".
The domain defines actions: `rotate`, `rotate-first-pass`, `rotate-first-pass-end`, `rotate-second-pass`, `rotate-second-pass-end`.
The `rotate` action initiates the process at a specific node `?n` with a rotation `?r`.
It changes the heading of `?n` and sets up the first pass cursor.
Then `rotate-first-pass` actions propagate the changes along the string.
Then `rotate-first-pass-end` transitions to the second pass.
Then `rotate-second-pass` actions propagate the changes (setting coordinates).
Then `rotate-second-pass-end` finishes.

The goal requires changing the shape of the string significantly.
However, looking at the domain description: "A rotation (fold) at node n consists of one action rotate and two following passes over to remaining part of string starting at n."
This implies a single rotation operation affects the string starting from a specific node `n`.
Usually, in these folding problems, you rotate the string at a specific joint to change the direction of the subsequent segments.
Here, the initial string is straight up. The goal is a zigzag.
To achieve a zigzag from a straight line, we likely need to perform rotations at specific nodes to bend the string.
However, the `rotate` action takes a node `?n` and a rotation `?r`.
The effect of `rotate` is:
- Change heading of `?n` from `?fromdir` to `?todir`.
- Set `(rotating)` and `(node-first-pass-next ?n ?r ?n)`.
This starts the propagation.
The propagation updates the headings and coordinates of the subsequent nodes `?n2`, `?n3`, etc.
The `rotate-first-pass` updates the heading of the next node `?n2` based on the rotation `?r` applied at `?nstart`.
Wait, the description says: "In the first pass, all directions are corrected according to the rotation applied...".
The `rotate-first-pass` action takes `?nstart`, `?r`, `?n1`, `?n2`.
Precondition: `CONNECTED ?n1 ?n2`, `node-first-pass-next ?nstart ?r ?n1`.
Effect: Updates `?n2`'s heading to `?n2setdir` (which is `NEXT-DIRECTION ?n2dir ?r ?n2setdir`).
Wait, `?n2dir` is the *current* heading of `?n2`. `?n2setdir` is the *new* heading.
So `rotate-first-pass` changes the heading of the next node in the chain based on the rotation applied at the start.
It also removes the old `at` fact and sets `free`.
Then `rotate-second-pass` sets the new `at` fact.

So, one `rotate` action + passes effectively rotates the entire suffix of the string starting at `?n` by `?r`.
If I want to change the shape from a straight line to a zigzag, I might need to rotate the string at multiple points.
However, the `rotate` action sets `(rotating)`. I cannot start another `rotate` while `(rotating)` is true.
So I must complete one full rotation sequence (rotate -> passes -> end) before starting another.
